image.alt: Painted scroll portrait of a seated lord in black robes holding a short baton, before a gold screen of mountain scenery

rounds.revealFact: Tokugawa Ieyasu outlasted his rivals to unify Japan and founded a shogunate that would keep the country at peace under his family for over 250 years.

image.alt: Colored drawing of ancient war chariots with archers charging forward, above rows of hieroglyphs

attribution.fileTitle: File:Ramses III (Ramesses II) lanciato col suo carro, e seguito da tre figli ugualmente sul carro, assale una fortezza piantata sopra una rupe, saettandone gli atterriti difensori (NYPL b14291206-425635).jpg
attribution.author: Scan by NYPL (Ippolito Rosellini)

clues: A young ruler riding at the head of his chariotry was nearly cut off when the enemy sprang an ambush, and had to rally his shaken troops in person.
The clash between two great powers ended not in conquest but in one of the earliest recorded peace treaties in history.

events.label: Leif Erikson's Norse crews reach the forests of North America
Zheng He sets sail with China's great treasure fleet
Bartolomeu Dias rounds the southern tip of Africa
James Cook charts the eastern coast of Australia

image.alt: Color photograph of a glowing orange fireball rising against a pitch-black sky

attribution.author: National Nuclear Security Administration / Nevada Site Office

eventQuestion.prompt: What is happening in this photograph?
options: The first test of a nuclear weapon

followUpQuestion.prompt: In which U.S. state did this take place?

rounds.prompt: A Visigothic army under Alaric sacks the city of Rome for the first time in centuries.
rounds.revealFact: Alaric's sack of Rome in 410 stunned the ancient world and became a symbol of Rome's accelerating decline in the west.

rounds.prompt: Where did a desert kingdom raise one of the world's largest cities of mud brick, a sprawl of nine great walled citadels?
rounds.revealFact: Chan Chan was the capital of the Chimú kingdom and the largest adobe city in the pre-Columbian Americas, until the Inca absorbed it in the 15th century.
